speaker: interviewer
text: --21st, 1995. We were talking about the train ride from Hamburg to Berlin in 1939 that you had taken to say goodbye to your aunt. And what had happened on that train?

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the train, I got into a conversation. The German trains are made-- like, they call them 'D trains.' The D means 'durchgehend'-- excuse me-- a through train. There's like, a corridor where you can walk. In other words, they're compartments. But off of the compartments are narrow corridors where people can walk through from one train to another, or down that whole train car. OK. So you stand out there. You get tired of sitting in your compartment. And you stand out there. And sometimes you'll strike up a conversation. And so there was a conversation struck. So I came out. I was 15 years old. I wasn't too bad-looking, but-- anyway, so these, these fellas started a conversation with me. You know, it was a very decent conversation. There was nothing wrong with it in any way, shape, or form. They weren't Jewish. They were in German Army uniforms. And you know, I was a German for all-- it didn't matter I said I was Jewish. But I was still German.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these people are just a few years older than I-- and started to talk about the maneuvers that they had been to, and that they were going to. And then-- and then one of them said, and, you know, I'm sure it'll be in August. And we're going to get ready to march into Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: He wasn't all enthused about it. But that's what he said. Well, it was September 1st that they marched into Poland. And the surprise-- the surprise is that everybody was so surprised. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, the, the, the press and all the people on the inside of what was going on, they must have known. They must have felt it. There were a lot of foreigners in Berlin and, and in other big German cities seeing what was going on. So-- and also seeing what was going on with the Jews. But nobody really did anything about it. And there was no hope that, that anyone would, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so then in-- what was the month that you left Hamburg to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left-- we left Hamburg on August 10th, 1939. The last night we spent in the apartment of my relatives, the Rothenburgs. In the meantime, the Frydas-- my mother's younger brother, Walter, his wife, and their two boys-- they had left for Australia the previous-- I believe it was May of 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had left. And then he was trying to sound hopeful to his sisters to try and do something over there to get them out of Germany. But, of course, that never occurred.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we-- in order to get to, to the ship that we were booked on to come to New York from Hamburg-- the ship left at a harbor which docked at a-- which-- excuse me-- docked at more or less the mouth of the Elbe River where it flows into the North Sea, but right at the North Sea which is called Cuxhaven, C-U-X-H-A-F-E-N. And so we had to take a train to go to Cuxhaven. We did not board the ship in Hamburg, which-- some of the ship lines also left from Hamburg. And we spent the last night there. We-- I think we slept on the floor. And even at that time, I, I even vaguely remember that night. I still couldn't quite get myself to believe that we were actually leaving the country. The fear that had done-- and I didn't bring this up very much earlier-- the fear was always that something will happen. They will find a way to keep us here by some chicanery. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took a taxicab from, from their apartment to the train station where we were going to board the train. And at, at the train station, we were almost ready to board the train to go to Cuxhaven. My cousin, Hans Joachin, came running.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came running, out of breath. He had already-- he was two years older. Those twins were two years older. They were 17 years old. They had already been inducted into some kind of work program for Jews only that the Nazis had created-- conjured up, created, whatever. And so he came running. I guess he just got enough time off. And he wanted to say goodbye to us. That was the last goodbye. And his last words were, I hope we can soon follow you. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That is the saddest thing, the saddest thing that I remember. [SOBBING] I can never get over that. [SOBBING] And we came here. And we could not do one darn thing to get them out of there, not one darn thing. My uncle was not willing to give any more affidavits, although he could have because nobody was living off of him. And these were people who would have worked and would have, you know, striven to maintain themselves without any handouts. He, he totally refused. And the Jewish agencies, or whatever it is that my parents ran to-- and I, I as well-- they were of no help at all. So we were never able-- we had no money. There were hardly any jobs in 1939. My father couldn't find a job at all. I, I got a job as nursemaid living somewhere where I got $3 a week and room and board. And my mother did housework. And so we, we were not able-- we had no money to say, this money is in escrow. We want to sign an affidavit and have part of the other-- of the family come over. That was totally impossible. And wherever we turned, there was absolutely no help. And I think that-- my mother never got over that. And I think that is something that-- I will never forget it. It's a very big lack within the Jewish community at that time. And was-- those were different days. Those-- it wasn't easy then, either. I know we came over here. And Marshall Field's didn't employee Jews. Montgomery Ward didn't employ Jews. General Motors didn't employ Jews. Those were different days. The Jewish community was not organized well. But still in all, there was just absolutely no way that we could get an affidavit for them. And that was the key.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the last time you had contact with family in Germany once you had come to the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When we came-- when we came, they had already sent postcards to, to us, care of my uncle's address. Because they didn't know where we were going to live. And they hoped that we would acclimate ourselves well. And so I have those here. I have them today. I have kept them. I don't know what will happen to them. Yes, and we, we had several more letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then we found out much later that the very first transport out of Hamburg to the east, to Poland-- I guess it was Poland-- Litzmannstadt. Lodz-- is that Poland? To the Polish ghetto in-- to the Jewish ghetto in Lodz-- that they were on the transport. And there were about a thousand Jews. The sad thing was that someone high up in the Jewish community had to select the names. I always wondered, how did they pick these names? That's a very sad chapter. I don't think anyone talks about it. But that's the-- yeah, well, they had to pick somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they had to have-- in case somebody didn't show up, or got sick, or what-- they had to have a thousand. So then they had to have 200 more in reserve in case they couldn't make the thousand. Then there were so many more transports after that. And my aunt in, in Berlin, from whom we received letters-- which I still have and which I showed you-- in-- throughout 1941, lamenting that the Rothenburgs had had to move to their new home, quote, unquote. You know, it had to be in a veiled language because by that time-- by the time the war started-- well, all of-- all of the letters were censored anyway. And the pity of it and the sadness of it is unspeakable and unbearable. And there's never a day that goes by that I don't think of it. But I try to-- I try to [LAUGHS] be a good person myself and maybe make up for those lives that, that just, you know, had to be ended there in this horrible, horrible way.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you never forget about it. It was-- it was a calculated, cold, bestial plan that was set up by these, these haters of Jews. Why they hated Jews in that way, who knows? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had seduced a whole people almost, so to speak, frightened the rest of them into going along with it-- of course, trying to keep the worst things secret, which did come out later. They did-- you know, everything was kept secret.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so anyway-- so we arrived here in New York City and-- on August 18, 1939. It was a sweltering hot day like today, [LAUGHS] except we weren't used to that kind of weather. My mother had-- wore a suit. I wore a suit. It was bad, bad, bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long until you got to Chicago? And then I'd like you to just tell me about, you know, what you did as a young person--

speaker: interviewee
text: Here.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Chicago until you became older and met your husband, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was a long time. But anyway, well-- and so we arrived in New York. There was no more Ellis Island business. So they came onboard ship from the US government. And they, they saw our papers. And they OKed everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we could go on land. And waiting for us was a cousin of my mother's, male, from her-- from where she was from in Westphalia. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he and his wife and his young son had come through Hamburg the day before-- and-- the year before-- I'm sorry-- and had settled in New York. They had relatives there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they wanted us-- they had already a very nice four-room apartment. And he was working for a Swiss yarn manufacturer, spinner and yarn. I don't know if it's even still on the market today. And he had a good position. And they wanted us to stay overnight or a couple of days. And I wanted to, to see New York. And-- however, we had orders from my uncle here to immediately get onto a Greyhound bus for which he had sent tickets and to, you know, do it that way. So we had no choice. I mean, there was no choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's move to Chicago now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go through a little bit in Chicago. So you came, you're-- three of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were picked up by my-- by the son-- or, or, or cousin of mine by another-- by another nephew of another sister of my father's. And we shared an apartment with them for a few weeks on Addison Street, as a matter of fact. And so then we got a small apartment on Briar Place later. My father could not find a job. My mother worked as a domestic. And my mother was not well. Both of my parents were then 49 years old. Now that seems young. I'm 71 now. But their health was not great. And I was 15 years old. I needed to go to school. Well, nobody was willing to have me go to school. Nobody was willing to, to see to it, you know, that I had a home to stay in and that I could go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we did the best we could, you know. We were glad we got out. We couldn't help our relatives there. And we could barely help ourselves here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we found out that a lot of people thought, when we were talking about Germany, that we were exaggerating, that nobody either cared about what the looming danger there really was-- probably didn't care. I don't know. That was a-- another psychological blow that was very hard to take. We also found there was a lot of anti-Semitism here, which was very hard to understand, too. Right-- my vision had been that this was a country that was, you know, just one wonderful country. And everything that was bad in Germany would be good here. And so-- OK. And I was 15 years old. Then I was 16 years old. And I-- my father made an attempt-- he had also cousins here who were born here to see if someone who would try to finance my going to school. I also was somewhat talented in drawing and things like that. I had always thought-- I had aspired to become a commercial artist. And, and I had a cousin who was an artist here. And however, there was nothing forthcoming in the way of any assistance or help or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we just did the best we could. And we got an apartment. Later on, we got a little larger apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I went to night school while I was working in a factory. I was working in a millinery factory. And then I was working in a-- I was working for Formfit, sewing bras. I was never good at sewing. I mean, I hated that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you go on and tell me then-- that was your one job. And then what did you do after that? I know you did some other things.

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, I went to school to learn. I went to school. I went to Jones Commercial High School, which had just more or less been set aside for, for that-- for business training by the Chicago school board. And I went at night. And I learned how to type. And I learned shorthand. And, and I got a job. And so I made-- or you know, it was a little nice living. I made a little bit more money. And then I got another job. And then, before long, my, my uncle wanted to hire me because I was so good at what I was doing. And I was so smart. And in the meantime-- in the meantime, I was 15 years old. I never had any other schooling after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what did you do--

speaker: interviewee
text: So then what did I do?

speaker: interviewer
text: --after-- before you met your husband. That's where we're heading now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I met my husband? Before I met my-- when I met my husband, I was working for my uncle as a bookkeeper's stenographer in his olive-importing firm on [? Ada ?] Street in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what year did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1949. That wasn't until 10 years later. And so we met, I think, in March, 1949. We got married in-- on November 24th, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Ernest George Bloch. And he was a nice guy, handsome. And we met actually at the-- what was 72 East 11th Street, which was a Jewish building. I had become very active in the National Council of Jewish Women, Junior Division. There was a junior division. And I made a lot of friends there. And I [LAUGHS] spent a lot of time in that building, working, you know, doing all kinds of things. And we had-- in the building, we had other Jewish functions going on. And there was a, a meeting of the Menorah Society of Northwestern University. And I was-- excuse me. In the meantime, I had gotten my high school certificate. That was by taking classes and by, by, by taking the German and using that for, for, for, for credit, and other things that I was very good at. And so I had that. What I did is I passed the entrance exam at the University of Illinois. And then I was taking courses at Northwestern University in the evening. And so I don't know if I joined the Menorah Society. The Menorah Society-- I don't even know if it's still there. It was neither a fraternity nor a sorority. It was a society. And so there was a program and-- being given at 72 East 11st Street. And that's where we met. And I was there with a girlfriend. And he said down next to me. And that's how we met.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many years have you been married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have been married 45 and 1/2 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have some children?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have some-- two wonderful daughters, I mean, wonderful. You couldn't ask for nicer. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names? And how old are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Andrea [? Emily-- ?] her middle name is [? Emily ?] after my aunt in Berlin. And she's 42. And Susan Joy is 39.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you-- as we're coming to a close during this taping-- I want to ask you, now that it is almost 50 years since the war has ended, is there a message or something you would like to leave for your children or future grandchildren? Any, any thoughts about that after all that you have been through and now that you reflect on those times?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think the important message is that we, who are privileged to live in a democracy, don't ever forget what a great privilege that is. We have to pay attention to whom we are electing. We must try-- even though we comfortably go from our day-to-day existence, and we are busy with our personal lives, we should never forget that we have to inform ourselves who these people are that are running for office. There are a lot of haters. They're out there. There's a lot of dangers that lurk out there. And I think that is one of the very, very important things, that you must involve yourself. Even if it's a little extra trouble, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you must know whom you are electing to office. And you must pay attention to what they're up to once they're in there. The other thing that I always came away with was that you don't blindly follow orders. You, you hear a lot of excuses that were given after the war was over in Germany. 'I was only following orders.' You don't blindly follow orders. I worked for a government-- a governmental-- not agency, but for a city government nearby for 22 years. And I never blindly followed [LAUGHS] orders. I mean, they weren't giving me orders like the Nazis did in Germany, but-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well I think those are-- I'm so glad you shared all that. Thank you so much for taping. It's been a pleasure. Thank you, Dorothea Bloch, for your taping.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. It's been sort of a catharsis, I think. It's very good for me, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Am I starting now? My, my father, Alfred Wallach, my mother, Helene Wallach, and I at the age of perhaps a year or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dorothea. And this picture was taken Hamburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1925. I had to think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, on the left from here, looking at it, is my mother's sister, Paula, my Aunt Paula Rothenburg; her husband, Max Rothenburg; his sister, whose name I do not remember-- Max's sister; and my aunt, my mother's sister-in-law, Walter Fryda's wife, Elsbeth, who went to Australia. In front, in the back of the four children, is my cousin Hans Joachin Rothenburg. And the little girl with long braids to the left was a cousin of theirs who was not related to me, whose name I don't remember. In the middle, the little boy is, is-- his name was Horst in Germany. Here is [? Fred ?] Fryda, Elsbeth, and Walter Fryda's son who lives in Australia. And to the right of him-- or actually, to the left of him in actuality-- is Hans Joachin's sister, Inge, Inge Rothenburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in a suburb of Hamburg. This is-- what as I was known then, as [? Thea ?] Wallach-- Dorothea Bloch now-- in my third year of school at the [NON-ENGLISH] school. And I-- all of our photographs were taken individually. It was in 1933. I thought the photographer had just snapped me. And so I moved my foot, and I made a dumb face. So that's what I thought.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my teacher-- am I talking? This was my fourth-grade class, my last year in that school, [NON-ENGLISH]. My teacher, [GERMAN] Rosenburg. And I'm at the rear with the blond, curly hair. And what year was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this again?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hamburg at the [NON-ENGLISH] School-- public school. Children's passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are we looking at?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, this was my children's passport, which served me to travel to Denmark in 1937. And it shows the shape of my face, which was oval. My eyes were gray, they claimed. And my hair was blond. And then there's my signature on the side. Issued by the police in Hamburg. This is the first page of my mother's passport when she needed to travel to the United States-- emigrate to the United States. It shows a big red J on the upper left hand corner, which stands for 'Jude,' or Jew. It gives my mother's last name and her maiden name. And then it gives-- it says, 'Helene Sara.' Sara was not her name. But every Jewish woman had to add the name of 'Sara' to her regular name. This is the, more or less, inside of my mother's passport, with her photo, again with her name and the 'Sara' added to it. It shows where she was born in Wattenscheid. It shows the day she was born. She lives in Hamburg. She had an oval face, blue-gray eyes, dark-blond hair, and no special markings. OK. This is a photo of my dear husband, Ernie, and myself taken in the fall of 1993 in Northbrook. It was actually taken in Northbrook. This is a photo of my two-- my two lovely daughters and my son-in-law and my son-in-law to be. This was our present at our last anniversary. At the top is Andrea [? Emily. ?] Her name is now Gabanski. Next to her in the checked shirt is my son-in-law, Gene Gabanski. Andrea is a personnel manager. Eugene is a software engineer. At the bottom is my wonderful daughter, Susan, who is soon going to get married. And Susan is a manager at Pricewaterhouse. And then, just above Susan is her fiance and soon-to-be husband. And that is-- and that is John Klepper. And John Klepper is an executive with a large candy company in Chicago. And that is the lovely other part of my family.
